Issue: Hey, I'm trying to set up a new test on HackerRank for our engineering hiring pipeline. I want to know if I can customize the coding environment — like letting candidates pick their preferred language? We hire for full-stack roles so they might want to use Python, JavaScript, or Java. Also, can I set a time limit per question rather than a global test timer?
Subject: Customizing coding test environment and per-question timers
expected_Response: Yes, HackerRank Screen allows you to configure multiple supported languages for a coding question, so candidates can choose their preferred language from the ones you enable. When creating or editing a question, you can select which languages are available for that question. Regarding timers, the platform supports a global test-level time limit. Per-question time limits are not a standard feature in the default setup — you may want to structure your test sections or reach out to your account manager to discuss advanced configuration options available on your plan.
agent_Response:
Here's what the corpus covers for your two questions:

**1. Language customization for candidates**
The retrieved articles don't include a direct step-by-step guide for enabling candidate language selection on a test. However, HackerRank's library does support questions in Python, JavaScript, and Java (and many others), and the platform supports full-stack/back-end question types. For specifics on how candidates choose or are restricted to certain languages within a test, this would need to be confirmed by the HackerRank support team, as the corpus doesn't cover that configuration path directly.

**2. Per-question time limits vs. a global timer**
The corpus covers section-level time settings but not individual per-question timers. From the Sections settings article, you can configure time limits at the section level. To do so:
1. Log in to your HackerRank for Work account.
2. Go to the Tests tab.
3. Open the test you want to update.
4. Go to Settings > Sections.
In the Sections settings panel, you can set section-level time limits, enable Time rollover (to carry unused time from one section to the next), and enable Revisit earlier sections (each section must have a defined time limit for this option). Per-question timers are not mentioned in the available documentation — a support agent can confirm whether that granularity is available.

For the language selection question, I'd recommend reaching out directly to HackerRank support for confirmation.

Issue: Hi, I'm trying to figure out how to invite candidates to a HackerRank screening test. I've created the test already but I can't find where to actually send the invites. Do I just copy a link or is there an email invite option? Also, is there a way to set a deadline so the link expires after a certain date?
Subject: How to invite candidates to a Screen test?
expected_Response: You can invite candidates to a HackerRank Screen test in a couple of ways. First, navigate to your test and look for the 'Invite Candidates' option — you can either enter candidate email addresses directly to send email invitations, or copy a shareable link to distribute yourself. For setting a deadline, you can configure an expiry date for the test so that the link becomes inactive after a specified date and time. These options are available in the test settings once your test has been created and saved.
agent_Response:
Both options are available — you can invite candidates via a shareable link or by sending individual email invites directly from HackerRank Screen.

For setting a deadline so the invitation expires after a certain date, here are the two ways to do it:

Option 1 — Set expiry when sending an invite to a specific candidate:
1. Click the Expiry dropdown.
2. Select an expiry option from the list.
   - If you choose Custom, select the expiry date, time, and time zone, and then click Save.

The selected expiry date appears on the invite.

Option 2 — Configure invite expiry at the test settings level (applies to all invites for that test):
1. Turn on the Invite Expiry toggle.
2. Choose one of the following options:
   - Set time period: Select Set time period, then enter the number of days the invite remains valid.
   - Set end date: Select Set end date, then choose the specific date on which the invite expires.

Note: By default, invite expiry is disabled at the test settings level.

The chunks retrieved cover the expiry/deadline side of your question in detail. However, the specific step-by-step flow for sending the initial email invite or copying a shareable link from within HackerRank Screen was not included in the retrieved articles. For that part, you may want to check the full 'Inviting Candidates to a Test' article at https://support.hackerrank.com/articles/6027855406-inviting-candidates-to-a-test or contact support@hackerrank.com.
agent_Justification: Grounded on 'Invite Candidates to a Test' (hackerrank/screen/invite-candidates/6027855406-inviting-candidates-to-a-test.md) for per-invite expiry steps, and 'Configure Test Invites Settings for Tests' (hackerrank/screen/test-settings/9544354489-configure-test-invites-settings-for-tests.md) for test-level invite expiry configuration. Issue: Does Claude have the ability to remember things between separate conversations? Like if I tell it my name and preferences in one chat, will it know that the next time I start a new conversation? I feel like it forgets everything each time which is kind of annoying for recurring tasks.
Subject: Does Claude remember previous conversations?
expected_Response: By default, Claude does not retain memory between separate conversations — each new conversation starts fresh without any context from previous chats. However, Claude does offer a Memory feature that can remember information across conversations when enabled. You can check your Claude settings to see if memory is available and enabled for your account. Within a single conversation, Claude will remember everything you've shared during that session.
agent_Response:
By default, Claude does not remember information between separate conversations — each new chat starts fresh without access to previous sessions.

However, memory features are available depending on your plan and how you're using Claude:

- Enterprise plan users can leverage memory, where Claude remembers relevant context from your chats and generates a memory summary. Incognito chats give you a way to exclude conversations from Claude's memory. More info: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11817273-using-claude-s-chat-search-and-memory-to-build-on-previous-context

- Memory is also available for Cowork projects, where Claude can remember context from tasks you've run in a project and apply it to future tasks in the same project. Note that memory is scoped to the project, so what Claude learns in one project doesn't carry over to others.

- For Claude Code users, the Auto-memory feature automatically saves preferences, corrections, and patterns between sessions. You can configure it by running /memory.

- Memory import/export is available for free, Pro, and Max plans on the web and Claude Desktop, letting you transfer memory between Claude and other AI providers.

The types of context Claude focuses on remembering include your role, projects, and professional context; communication preferences and working style; technical preferences and coding style; and project details and ongoing work.

If you're on a plan that supports memory, you can find more details at https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11817273-use-claude-s-chat-search-and-memory-to-build-on-previous-context
agent_Justification: Grounded on multiple chunks: 'Use Claude's chat search and memory to build on previous context' (claude/claude/conversation-management/11817273), 'Organize your tasks with projects in Claude Cowork' (claude/claude/features-and-capabilities/14116274), 'Release notes' (claude/claude/12138966), 'Import and export your memory from Claude' (claude/claude/features-and-capabilities/12123587), and 'Claude Code power user tips' (claude/claude-code/launch-guides/14554000). Chunks collectively explain that cross-session memory is not the default but is available on specific plans and contexts.

We want candidates to work with our actual tech stack rather than generic problems.
Subject: Adding custom questions to HackerRank question library
expected_Response: Yes, HackerRank does support custom questions. In the HackerRank for Work platform, you can create your own custom coding challenges and add them to your question library. This allows you to write questions tailored to your specific tech stack, frameworks, or internal scenarios. Once created, these custom questions can be added to any test you build, just like questions from the standard library. agent_Response:
Yes, HackerRank supports custom question creation. You can create and save your own coding questions, including questions tailored to your internal tech stack or proprietary frameworks, and store them in your personal or team library.

When browsing questions during an interview or test setup, you'll see these library tabs:
- HackerRank Library: questions from HackerRank's standard library
- My Company: questions your company has shared or created
- My Questions: custom questions you personally created

You can also create and save custom scratchpad questions directly during a live interview and reuse them in future sessions. This works across both coding and whiteboard questions, so your content is always in one place.

For question types that need custom scoring logic (such as approximate solution questions), you can define your own scoring via a custom checker. Once saved, the question appears under "My Company" questions in the HackerRank Library.

For more details on adding questions to interviews, see: https://support.hackerrank.com/articles/6761309830

Second, do candidates see their score immediately after submitting, or is that controlled by the admin? Third, is there a way to prevent candidates from copy-pasting code into the editor?
Subject: HackerRank Screen - time limits, score visibility, copy-paste restriction
expected_Response: Great questions about HackerRank Screen! Here's what you need to know: (1) Time limits can be set at the overall test level; individual question-level time limits are not a standard configuration option in most Screen plans, so the timer applies to the full assessment. (2) Score visibility after submission is controlled by the admin — you can configure whether candidates see their results or not from the test settings. (3) HackerRank Screen does have a plagiarism detection and proctoring feature that includes copy-paste monitoring; you can enable settings to flag or restrict copy-paste activity from outside the browser. Check your test's proctoring settings to turn this on. If you need specifics based on your plan, our support team can walk you through what's available on your account.

**2. Candidate score visibility after submission**
The retrieved articles don't directly address whether candidates see their scores immediately after submitting or whether that setting is admin-controlled. This also needs a human to confirm.

**3. Preventing copy-paste into the editor**
There are two mechanisms available:

- **Desktop App Mode**: Copy-paste functionality is fully disabled to prevent pasting content from external sources into the editor (hackerrank/screen/test-integrity/5973590014-hackerrank-desktop-app-mode.md).
- **Proctor Mode**: Also disables copy-paste functionality to prevent pasting content from external sources into the editor (hackerrank/screen/test-integrity/5663779659-proctor-mode.md).
- **Copy-Paste Tracking (default)**: Copy-paste tracking is enabled by default for all customer tests. This deters candidates from pasting code and records Copy-Paste Frequency in the Excel report and individual candidate reports, so reviewers can see lines of code copy-pasted (hackerrank/screen/best-practice-guides/4291690360-plagiarism-best-practices-guide.md).

For the first two questions, a support agent will need to follow up with the specifics.

expected_Response: Great that you're planning ahead! Here's some guidance for using your Visa card in Japan: (1) Travel notice: It's generally a good idea to notify your card-issuing bank before international travel so they don't flag your transactions as suspicious and block the card — check your bank's app or call the number on the back of your card to set a travel notice. (2) Foreign transaction fees: These are set by your individual card issuer, not Visa directly. Review your card's terms or call your bank to find out if a foreign transaction fee applies and what the rate is. (3) Acceptance in Japan: Visa is widely accepted in Japan at major hotels, department stores, and larger retailers. However, Japan is still a cash-heavy country in many contexts — smaller restaurants, local shops, temples, and vending machines may be cash-only. It's advisable to carry some Japanese yen alongside your card.
